content:
# Leviticus Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68v–69r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_018_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_018_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 104–105 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 18 opens the central section of the Holiness Code (chapters 18–27), a body of legislation whose governing principle is holiness: "You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" (19:2). Chapter 18 itself is framed with exceptional literary care. It opens with a double prohibition: do not act as Egypt acted, do not act as Canaan acts (vv.1–5). The positive counter-command is va-chai bahem — "he shall live by them" (v.5), one of the most theologically weighty phrases in the entire Torah, cited by Ezekiel (20:11, 13, 21), Nehemiah (9:29), and Paul (Gal 3:12; Rom 10:5) as the purpose of the commandments. The body of the chapter (vv.6–23) consists of sexual prohibitions arranged in loose order from family relations (vv.6–18) outward to community violations (vv.19–23). The closing frame (vv.24–30) uses the extraordinary image of a land that has been defiled to the point of vomiting out its inhabitants — va-taqi ha-aretz, "the land vomited out" — as the theological ground for the entire prohibition list. The prohibitions are stated as lo tiqrevu, "you shall not approach to uncover nakedness" (v.6), where the idiom of "uncovering nakedness" (gallot ervah) encompasses both exposure and sexual intercourse. The chapter contains the three most contested verses in the Levitical sexual code: v.21 (passing children through to Molech), v.22 (male homosexual intercourse, to'evah, "abomination"), and v.23 (bestiality, tevel, "confusion/perversion"). The chapter's key Hebrew term is ervah [עֶרְוָה] — "nakedness, genitalia, sexual exposure" — which occurs 24 times, more than in any other chapter of the Hebrew Bible. This concentration of the word is not accidental: the entire chapter is about boundaries, boundaries that, when crossed, produce defilement so severe that the land itself expels the violators as a body expels something toxic.]*

---

**1** And the LORD [YHWH] spoke [vayedabber] [vayedabber — "and he spoke"; the standard narrative introduction to a new divine speech unit in Leviticus; qal imperfect with waw-consecutive; LXX: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος (Kai elalēsen kyrios, "And the LORD spoke")] to Moshe [el-Moshe] [el-Moshe — "to Moshe"; the direct address formula; as throughout Leviticus; LXX: πρὸς Μωυσῆν (pros Mōysēn, "to Moshe")], saying [lemor] [lemor — "saying"; the infinitive construct introducing quoted divine speech; LXX: λέγων (legōn, "saying")]:

**2** "Speak [dabber] [dabber — "speak"; piel imperative: Moshe is commanded to speak; LXX: Λάλησον (Lalēson, "Speak")] to the sons of Yisra'el [el benei Yisra'el] [el benei Yisra'el — "to the sons of Yisra'el"; the entire Israelite community; contrast chapter 17's address which opened with Aharon and his sons as well; here the legislation is directed to the whole people; LXX: πρὸς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ (pros huious Israēl, "to the sons of Yisra'el")] and you shall say to them [ve-amarta alehem] [ve-amarta alehem — "and you shall say to them"; the double speech formula dabber...ve-amarta is characteristic of the Holiness Code; LXX: καὶ ἐρεῖς πρὸς αὐτούς (kai ereis pros autous, "and you shall say to them")]: 'I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikhem] [ani YHWH Eloheikhem — "I am the LORD your God"; the divine self-identification formula; ani = "I"; YHWH = the covenant name; Eloheikhem = "your God" (plural suffix — addressing the community); this formula is the theological foundation of the Holiness Code's authority — the commandments derive from who YHWH is in relation to Yisra'el; LXX: Ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν (Egō eimi kyrios ho theos hymōn, "I am the LORD your God")]:

**3** As the doings of the land of Egypt [ke-ma'aseh eretz Mitzrayim] [ke-ma'aseh eretz Mitzrayim — "according to the deeds/doings of the land of Egypt"; ke- = "as, like, according to"; ma'aseh = "deed, doing, practice, act, work"; eretz Mitzrayim = "the land of Egypt"; the ma'aseh is the entire pattern of behavior, the customary practices of a culture; LXX: κατὰ τὰ ἐπιτηδεύματα γῆς Αἰγύπτου (kata ta epitēdeumata gēs Aigyptou, "according to the practices of the land of Egypt"), using ἐπιτήδευμα (epitēdeuma, "practice, pursuit, habit of life")] in which you dwelt [asher yeshavtem bah] [asher yeshavtem bah — "in which you dwelt / in which you lived"; qal perfect plural; bah = "in it" (feminine, referring to eretz); LXX: ἐν ᾗ κατῳκήσατε ἐν αὐτῇ (en hē katōkēsate en autē, "in which you dwelt in it")] — you shall not do [lo' ta'asu] [lo' ta'asu — "you shall not do"; qal imperfect plural with the negative lo': the categorical prohibition; LXX: οὐ ποιήσετε (ou poiēsete, "you shall not do")]. And as the doings of the land of Canaan [u-che-ma'aseh eretz Kena'an] [u-che-ma'aseh eretz Kena'an — "and according to the doings of the land of Canaan"; u- = "and"; the same formula as for Egypt; Kena'an = the land into which Yisra'el will enter; both past home and future home are placed outside the bounds of permitted behavior; LXX: καὶ κατὰ τὰ ἐπιτηδεύματα γῆς Χανααν (kai kata ta epitēdeumata gēs Chanaan, "and according to the practices of the land of Canaan")] to which I am bringing you [asher ani mevi' etkhem shamah] [asher ani mevi' etkhem shamah — "to which I am bringing you there"; mevi' = hiphil participle of bo' ("to come, to enter"): "I am causing you to come"; shamah = "there, thither"; a present-tense divine action: the bringing is ongoing, in process; LXX: εἰς ἣν εἰσάγω ὑμᾶς ἐκεῖ (eis hēn eisagō hymas ekei, "into which I am bringing you there")] — you shall not do [lo' ta'asu] [lo' ta'asu — as above; both civilizations are equally excluded as behavioral models; LXX: οὐ ποιήσετε (ou poiēsete, "you shall not do")]. And in their statutes [u-be-chuqqoteihem] [u-be-chuqqoteihem — "and in their statutes / by their prescribed practices"; chuqqot = "statutes, ordinances, engraved prescriptions" (from chaqaq, "to engrave, to prescribe"); the suffix -hem = "their"; to "walk in the statutes" of another people is to adopt their entire cultic-social regulatory system; LXX: καὶ ἐν τοῖς νομίμοις αὐτῶν (kai en tois nomimois autōn, "and in their ordinances/statutes"), using νόμιμος (nomimos, "lawful, statutory, prescribed")] you shall not walk [lo' telekhun] [lo' telekhun — "you shall not walk / you shall not go"; qal imperfect plural; halakh be- = "to walk in, to conduct oneself according to"; the walking metaphor for living out a pattern of life is characteristic of the Hebrew Bible; LXX: οὐ πορεύσεσθε (ou poreuesthe, "you shall not walk/proceed")]:

**4** My judgments [et-mishpatai] [et-mishpatai — "my judgments / my justice-decisions"; mishpatim [מִשְׁפָּטִים] = "judgments, decisions, ordinances, laws" (from shaphat, "to judge"); the mishpatim are reasoned judgments derived from principles — often presented as case law ("if... then..."), as distinguished from chuqqot (engraved statutes) which may have no stated rationale; LXX: τὰ κρίματά μου (ta krimata mou, "my judgments/decisions"), using κρίμα (krima, "judgment, decision")] you shall do [ta'asu] [ta'asu — "you shall do"; the positive command; LXX: ποιήσετε (poiēsete, "you shall do")] and my statutes [ve-et-chuqqotai] [ve-et-chuqqotai — "and my statutes"; the pairing mishpatim + chuqqot covers the full range of divine legislation: case-judgments plus absolute prescriptions; LXX: τὰ προστάγματά μου (ta prostagmata mou, "my commands/ordinances"), using πρόσταγμα (prostagma, "command, injunction, ordinance") — the LXX renders chuqqot as prostagmata ("commands") rather than nomima/dikaiōmata here] you shall keep [tishmeru] [tishmeru — "you shall keep / you shall guard"; qal imperfect plural of shamar: "to keep, to watch over, to observe, to guard"; shamar denotes the active maintenance and protection of an obligation; LXX: φυλάξεσθε (phylaxesthe, "you shall guard/keep"), using φυλάσσω (phylassō, "to guard, to watch, to keep")] to walk in them [la-lekhet bahem] [la-lekhet bahem — "to walk in them / to conduct yourselves by them"; the walking metaphor as in v.3; LXX: πορεύεσθαι ἐν αὐτοῖς (poreuesthai en autois, "to walk/conduct yourselves in them")]. I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikhem] [ani YHWH Eloheikhem — the formula repeated; this bracketing of the command "do my judgments, keep my statutes" with the divine self-identification is characteristic Holiness Code style; LXX: Ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν (Egō eimi kyrios ho theos hymōn, "I am the LORD your God")]:

**5** And you shall keep my statutes [u-shmartem et-chuqqotai] [u-shmartem et-chuqqotai — "and you shall keep my statutes"; the hortatory summary before the climactic positive statement; LXX: καὶ φυλάξεσθε πάντα τὰ προστάγματά μου (kai phylaxesthe panta ta prostagmata mou, "and you shall keep all my commands") — the LXX adds "all" (panta) not present in MT] and my judgments [ve-et-mishpatai] [ve-et-mishpatai — "and my judgments"; the reversal of the order from v.4 (mishpatim first there, chuqqot first here) is characteristic of Hebrew ring-composition; LXX: καὶ τὰ κρίματά μου (kai ta krimata mou, "and my judgments")] which a man shall do [asher ya'aseh otam ha-adam] [asher ya'aseh otam ha-adam — "which a man shall do them / which the human being does them"; ya'aseh = qal imperfect of 'asah: "shall do, shall perform, shall carry out"; ha-adam = "the man, the human being, humanity" (the generic singular — not ha-ish "a specific man" but ha-adam, "the human being as type"); the use of ha-adam may gesture toward the universal-human scope of the commandments, though in context they are addressed to Yisra'el; LXX: ἃ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος (ha poiēsas auta anthrōpos, "which a man having done them"), using ἄνθρωπος (anthrōpos, "human being, man")] — and he shall live by them [va-chai bahem] [va-chai bahem — "and he shall live by them / and he shall live through them"; va-chai = waw-consecutive + qal perfect of chayah: "and he shall live"; bahem = "by them, through them, in them"; the phrase is the hinge of the entire verse and one of the most theologically loaded clauses in the Torah. The question is whether chai means (a) live = have biological life, survive; (b) live = truly/fully live, flourish; or (c) live = have eschatological/eternal life. In context, the statement is a promise: doing the commandments leads to life. Ezekiel cites this verse three times (20:11, 13, 21) in his history of Yisra'el's failure. Paul cites it in Gal 3:12 ("the law is not of faith, but 'the one who does them shall live by them'") as an antithesis to faith-righteousness, and in Rom 10:5 as Moses' description of the "righteousness that is based on the law." The contrast Paul draws between chai bahem (life through doing) and the righteousness of faith does not disparage the verse — it establishes the verse's claim as real and as high: life is genuinely offered through obedience. LXX: καὶ ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς (kai zēsetai en autois, "and he shall live in them"), using ζάω (zaō, "to live") — the LXX preposition is ἐν (en, "in") where the Hebrew is ba- ("by/through/in")] — I am the LORD [ani YHWH] [ani YHWH — "I am the LORD"; the shorter form here (without Eloheikhem) closes the frame before the list of prohibited relations begins; LXX: Ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν (Egō eimi kyrios ho theos hymōn, "I am the LORD your God") — the LXX adds "your God" which is absent from the MT here]:

---

**6** Any man [ish ish] [ish ish — "a man, a man"; the distributive doubling as in 17:3, 8, 10, 13: "any man whatsoever"; the legal formulation of universal applicability; LXX: Ἄνθρωπος ἄνθρωπος (Anthrōpos anthrōpos, "any man")] — to any of his close flesh [el-kol she'er besaro] [el-kol she'er besaro — "to any remnant/flesh of his flesh / to any of his near-of-kin"; she'er [שְׁאֵר] = "remaining portion, near-flesh, close kin" (from sha'ar, "to remain"); she'er basar = "near kin" or literally "flesh-remainder" — the flesh-connection that remains between persons through bloodline or marriage; the full term she'er besaro = "the near-kin of his flesh / his flesh-kin"; LXX: πρὸς πάντα οἰκεῖα σαρκὸς αὐτοῦ (pros panta oikeia sarkos autou, "to all the intimates of his flesh"), using οἰκεῖος (oikeios, "of the household, intimate, akin")] you shall not approach [lo' tiqrevu] [lo' tiqrevu — "you shall not approach"; qal imperfect plural of qarav: "to draw near, to approach"; the prohibition is of approach, not merely of the act itself — distance is required from the outset; the euphemistic idiom lo' tiqrevu le-gallot ervah ("you shall not approach to uncover nakedness") encompasses both foreplay/exposure and sexual intercourse; LXX: οὐ προσελεύσεσθε (ou proseleuσesthe, "you shall not approach"), using προσέρχομαι (proserchomai, "to come to, to approach")] to uncover nakedness [le-gallot ervah] [le-gallot ervah — "to uncover nakedness / to expose genitalia"; le- = infinitive of purpose ("in order to"); gallot = piel infinitive of galah: "to uncover, to expose, to strip bare"; ervah [עֶרְוָה] = "nakedness, genital exposure, sexual shame" (from 'arah, "to be bare, to be empty"); the technical phrase gallot ervah ("uncover nakedness") is used 24 times in this chapter alone, making it the chapter's central idiom; in context, the uncovering of a person's ervah means engaging in sexual intercourse with them; the phrase may also include the broader sense of exposing another person's shame or violating their boundaries; LXX: ἀποκαλύψαι ἀσχημοσύνην (apokalypsai aschēmosynēn, "to uncover/reveal indecency/nakedness"), using ἀποκαλύπτω (apokalyptō, "to uncover, to reveal") and ἀσχημοσύνη (aschēmosynē, "indecency, unseemliness, nakedness") — the LXX's ἀσχημοσύνη ("unseemliness, indecency") adds a note of moral disgrace that is more evaluative than the neutral Hebrew ervah ("nakedness")]. I am the LORD [ani YHWH] [ani YHWH — "I am the LORD"; the divine self-identification punctuates not only the frame (vv.2, 4, 5) but also individual prohibitions throughout the chapter; LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος (egō eimi kyrios, "I am the LORD")]:

**7** The nakedness of your father [ervat avikha] [ervat avikha — "nakedness of your father"; the construct chain ervat + noun throughout vv.7–18 identifies whose nakedness is prohibited; avikha = "your father" (second person singular possessive); LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην πατρός σου (aschēmosynēn patros sou, "the nakedness of your father")] and the nakedness of your mother [ve-ervat immekha] [ve-ervat immekha — "and the nakedness of your mother"; immekha = "your mother"; the father's nakedness and the mother's nakedness are here equated — the mother's nakedness IS the father's nakedness by the logic of marital union (they are "one flesh," Gen 2:24); hence having intercourse with one's mother violates the father; LXX: καὶ ἀσχημοσύνην μητρός σου (kai aschēmosynēn mētros sou, "and the nakedness of your mother")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] [lo' tegalleh — "you shall not uncover"; piel imperfect of galah with negative lo': the categorical prohibition; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις (ouk apokalypseis, "you shall not uncover/reveal")]. She is your mother [immekha hi'] [immekha hi' — "she is your mother"; the identity assertion as ground for the prohibition; hi' = "she is" (the copular feminine pronoun); LXX: μητέρα σου (mētera sou, "your mother") — the LXX lacks the explicit copular assertion, rendering it more baldly: "[the nakedness] of your mother"]; you shall not uncover her nakedness [lo' tegalleh ervatah] [lo' tegalleh ervatah — "you shall not uncover her nakedness"; the prohibition repeated with the pronominal suffix referring to the mother; the repetition emphasizes the gravity of the prohibition; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (ouk apokalypseis tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "you shall not uncover her nakedness")]:

**8** The nakedness of your father's wife [ervat eshet avikha] [ervat eshet avikha — "nakedness of wife of your father"; eshet = "wife of" (construct of ishah, "woman/wife"); the prohibition addresses the stepmother specifically — not the mother of the addressee but any additional wife of his father; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην γυναικὸς πατρός σου (aschēmosynēn gynaikos patros sou, "nakedness of the wife of your father")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — it is your father's nakedness [ervat avikha hi'] [ervat avikha hi' — "it is your father's nakedness"; the ground for the prohibition: the stepmother's nakedness belongs to the father's relational sphere; to uncover it is to violate him; hi' = "it is"; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνη πατρός σου ἐστίν (aschēmosynē patros sou estin, "it is the nakedness of your father")]:

**9** The nakedness of your sister [ervat achotekha] [ervat achotekha — "nakedness of your sister"; achotekha = "your sister"; the prohibition applies regardless of the parental relationship, as the following clause specifies; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην ἀδελφῆς σου (aschēmosynēn adelphēs sou, "nakedness of your sister")] — the daughter of your father [bat-avikha] [bat-avikha — "daughter of your father"; bat = "daughter"; the paternal half-sister; LXX: θυγατρὸς πατρός σου (thygatros patros sou, "daughter of your father")] or the daughter of your mother [o bat-immekha] [o bat-immekha — "or daughter of your mother"; the maternal half-sister; thus the prohibition covers both paternal and maternal half-sisters; LXX: ἢ θυγατρὸς μητρός σου (ē thygatros mētros sou, "or daughter of your mother")] — whether born at home [yeledet bayit] [yeledet bayit — "born of the house / born at home"; yeledet = "born" (qal participle feminine); bayit = "house, household"; a child born within the family household as opposed to born outside (a child of the same father by a different wife's household?); the precise distinction is between a child born into the domestic unit and one born outside it; LXX: ἢ τῆς ἔνδον γεννηθείσης (ē tēs endon gennētheisēs, "or of the one born within")] or born outside [o yeledet chuts] [o yeledet chuts — "or born outside"; chuts = "outside, exterior, abroad"; a child born of the same blood relationship but in a different household (e.g., of a divorced mother's subsequent family?); LXX: ἢ τῆς ἔξω γεννηθείσης (ē tēs exō gennētheisēs, "or of the one born outside")] — you shall not uncover their nakedness [lo' tegalleh ervatam] [lo' tegalleh ervatam — "you shall not uncover their nakedness"; the plural suffix -am covers all the cases listed; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (ouk apokalypseis tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "you shall not uncover her nakedness") — the LXX uses the singular feminine suffix "her" rather than the MT plural "their"; a textual divergence]:

**10** The nakedness of your son's daughter [ervat bat-binkha] [ervat bat-binkha — "nakedness of daughter of your son"; bat = "daughter"; binkha = "your son"; the granddaughter through the male line; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην θυγατρὸς υἱοῦ σου (aschēmosynēn thygatros huiou sou, "nakedness of the daughter of your son")] or the daughter of your daughter [o bat-bitkha] [o bat-bitkha — "or daughter of your daughter"; the granddaughter through the female line; together: the prohibition covers all granddaughters; LXX: ἢ θυγατρὸς θυγατρός σου (ē thygatros thygatros sou, "or daughter of your daughter")] — you shall not uncover their nakedness [lo' tegalleh ervatam] [lo' tegalleh ervatam — "you shall not uncover their nakedness"; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῶν (ouk apokalypseis tēn aschēmosynēn autōn, "you shall not uncover their nakedness")] — for their nakedness [ki ervatekha hennah] [ki ervatekha hennah — "for they are your nakedness / for it is your nakedness"; ki = "for, because"; ervatekha = "your nakedness" (singular — the nakedness of the addressee himself); hennah = "they are" (third person feminine plural); the ground clause: the granddaughters are the man's own nakedness — they are of his flesh; to uncover theirs is to expose his own; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνη σού εἰσιν (aschēmosynē sou eisin, "they are your nakedness")]:

**11** The nakedness of your father's wife's daughter [ervat bat-eshet avikha] [ervat bat-eshet avikha — "nakedness of daughter of wife of your father"; this is the stepsister — the daughter of one's father's wife by a previous husband or by the same father; i.e., a half-sister born of one's father by a different woman who was or became his wife; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην θυγατρὸς γυναικὸς πατρός σου (aschēmosynēn thygatros gynaikos patros sou, "nakedness of the daughter of the wife of your father")] — begotten of your father [moladat avikha] [moladat avikha — "begotten/born of your father / the offspring of your father"; moladat = "offspring, birth, begetting" (from yalad, "to beget, to bear"); moladat avikha = "the offspring of your father, born of your father"; the relative clause specifying that this is not a stepsister from the wife's previous relationship but a half-sister sharing the same father; thus: the half-sister (same father, different mother); LXX: τεχθεῖσα ἐκ πατρός σου (techtheisa ek patros sou, "born from your father")] — she is your sister [achotkha hi'] [achotkha hi' — "she is your sister"; the identity assertion as in v.7; LXX: ἀδελφή σου ἐστίν (adelphē sou estin, "she is your sister")] — you shall not uncover her nakedness [lo' tegalleh ervatah] [lo' tegalleh ervatah — "you shall not uncover her nakedness"; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (ouk apokalypseis tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "you shall not uncover her nakedness")]:

**12** The nakedness of your father's sister [ervat achat avikha] [ervat achat avikha — "nakedness of sister of your father"; achat = "sister"; the paternal aunt; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην ἀδελφῆς πατρός σου (aschēmosynēn adelphēs patros sou, "nakedness of the sister of your father")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — she is the near-kin of your father [she'er avikha hi'] [she'er avikha hi' — "she is the flesh-kin of your father / she is the near-relative of your father"; she'er = "near kin, flesh-kin" as in v.6; hi' = "she is"; LXX: οἰκεία πατρός σου ἐστίν (oikeia patros sou estin, "she is the kinswoman/intimate of your father"), using οἰκεῖος (oikeios, "of the household, intimate, kindred")]:

**13** The nakedness of your mother's sister [ervat achat immekha] [ervat achat immekha — "nakedness of sister of your mother"; the maternal aunt; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην ἀδελφῆς μητρός σου (aschēmosynēn adelphēs mētros sou, "nakedness of the sister of your mother")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — for she is the near-kin of your mother [ki she'er immekha hi'] [ki she'er immekha hi' — "for she is the flesh-kin of your mother"; the parallel formulation to v.12; LXX: οἰκεία γὰρ μητρός σου ἐστίν (oikeia gar mētros sou estin, "for she is the kinswoman of your mother")]:

**14** The nakedness of your father's brother [ervat achi avikha] [ervat achi avikha — "nakedness of brother of your father"; the paternal uncle; achi = "brother of" (construct); the prohibition here is not about the uncle but about his wife (v.14b); by uncovering the uncle's wife's nakedness one exposes the uncle's nakedness; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην ἀδελφοῦ πατρός σου (aschēmosynēn adelphou patros sou, "nakedness of the brother of your father")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — you shall not approach his wife [el-ishto lo' tiqrav] [el-ishto lo' tiqrav — "to his wife you shall not approach"; the idiom is the same lo' tiqrav ("you shall not approach") as in v.6; LXX: πρὸς τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ οὐκ εἰσελεύσῃ (pros tēn gynaika autou ouk eiseleusē, "to his wife you shall not enter/go") — the LXX uses "enter" rather than "approach"]; she is your aunt [dodatkha hi'] [dodatkha hi' — "she is your aunt / your father's brother's wife"; dodah [דּוֹדָה] = "aunt" — specifically the wife of one's father's brother (as opposed to one's father's sister, who is called achat avikha in v.12); dod [דּוֹד] = "uncle (paternal), beloved" (from the same root as dodim, "love, lovemaking"); thus dodatkha hi' = "she is your beloved's-wife, your father's-brother's wife"; LXX: αὕτη γυνὴ τοῦ πατραδέλφου σου ἐστίν (hautē gynē tou patradelphou sou estin, "she is the wife of your father's brother"), using πατράδελφος (pathradelphos, "father's brother, paternal uncle")]:

**15** The nakedness of your daughter-in-law [ervat kallatkha] [ervat kallatkha — "nakedness of your daughter-in-law"; kallah [כַּלָּה] = "bride, daughter-in-law" (the standard term for the wife of one's son); LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην νύμφης σου (aschēmosynēn nymphēs sou, "nakedness of your bride/daughter-in-law"), using νύμφη (nymphē, "bride, young woman, daughter-in-law")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — she is the wife of your son [eshet binkha hi'] [eshet binkha hi' — "she is the wife of your son"; the identity-grounding clause; LXX: γυνὴ γὰρ υἱοῦ σου ἐστίν (gynē gar huiou sou estin, "for she is the wife of your son")]; you shall not uncover her nakedness [lo' tegalleh ervatah] [lo' tegalleh ervatah — "you shall not uncover her nakedness"; the double prohibition marks the particular gravity of this offense; LXX: οὐκ ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (ouk apokalypseis tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "you shall not uncover her nakedness")]:

**16** The nakedness of your brother's wife [ervat eshet achikha] [ervat eshet achikha — "nakedness of wife of your brother"; achikha = "your brother"; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην γυναικὸς ἀδελφοῦ σου (aschēmosynēn gynaikos adelphou sou, "nakedness of the wife of your brother")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — it is your brother's nakedness [ervat achikha hi'] [ervat achikha hi' — "it is your brother's nakedness"; the grounding clause; hi' = "it is"; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνη ἀδελφοῦ σου ἐστίν (aschēmosynē adelphou sou estin, "it is the nakedness of your brother") — note: the exception for levirate marriage (Deut 25:5–10) is not stated here; this chapter presents the absolute prohibition; the levirate duty is a later, exceptional case where the normal rule is suspended for covenant-family purposes]:

**17** The nakedness of a woman and her daughter [ervat ishah u-vitkha] [ervat ishah u-vitkha — "nakedness of a woman and her daughter"; the prohibition of a woman and her daughter simultaneously — not a prohibition on the woman or her daughter individually but on taking both; LXX: ἀσχημοσύνην γυναικὸς καὶ θυγατρὸς αὐτῆς (aschēmosynēn gynaikos kai thygatros autēs, "nakedness of a woman and her daughter")] you shall not uncover [lo' tegalleh] — you shall not take [lo' tiqach] [lo' tiqach — "you shall not take"; qal imperfect of laqach: "to take (in marriage)"; the verb for acquiring a wife in a legal marriage; LXX: οὐ λήμψῃ (ou lēmpsē, "you shall not take")] the daughter of her son [et-bat-benhah] [et-bat-benhah — "the daughter of her son"; the granddaughter through a previous son; LXX: τὴν θυγατέρα τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς (tēn thygatera tou huiou autēs, "the daughter of her son")] or the daughter of her daughter [ve-et-bat-bitkha] [ve-et-bat-bitkha — "and the daughter of her daughter"; the granddaughter through a daughter; together: one may not marry a woman and her daughter, granddaughter by son, or granddaughter by daughter; LXX: καὶ τὴν θυγατέρα τῆς θυγατρὸς αὐτῆς (kai tēn thygatera tēs thygatros autēs, "and the daughter of her daughter")] to uncover her nakedness [le-gallot ervatah] [le-gallot ervatah — "to uncover her nakedness"; the infinitive of purpose; LXX: ἀποκαλύψαι τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (apokalypsai tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "to uncover her nakedness")] — they are her near-kin [she'erah hennah] [she'erah hennah — "they are her near-kin / they are her flesh-kin"; she'er + possessive suffix -ah ("her"); hennah = "they are" (feminine plural); the granddaughters are the flesh-kin of the woman; to take them is to violate the bonds of flesh-connection; LXX: οἰκεῖαί εἰσιν (oikeiai eisin, "they are kinswomen/intimates")] — it is wickedness [zimmah hi'] [zimmah hi' — "it is wickedness / it is an outrage"; zimmah [זִמָּה] = "wicked scheme, outrage, depravity, lewdness"; a stronger evaluative term than to'evah (v.22) or tevel (v.23) in the sense that zimmah denotes a deliberate, plotting moral outrage; LXX: ἀσέβεια ἐστίν (asebeia estin, "it is impiety/godlessness"), using ἀσέβεια (asebeia, "impiety, ungodliness, irreverence toward the divine") — the LXX's ἀσέβεια elevates the offense to the theological realm of anti-divine action]:

**18** And a woman in addition to her sister [ve-ishah el-achoteha] [ve-ishah el-achoteha — "and a woman alongside/in addition to her sister"; the idiom ishah el-achotah ("a woman to/alongside her sister") appears in Exod 26:3, 5, 6 of curtains joined "one to another" — the idiom means "each to its companion, one in addition to/alongside another"; here it means: in addition to the first wife, you shall not marry her sister (as a second/rival wife); LXX: γυναῖκα ἐπ᾽ ἀδελφῇ αὐτῆς (gynaika ep' adelphē autēs, "a woman upon/in addition to her sister")] you shall not take [lo' tiqach] [lo' tiqach — "you shall not take"; as in v.17; LXX: οὐ λήμψῃ (ou lēmpsē, "you shall not take")] to make her a rival [li-tsror] [li-tsror — "to make her a rival / to cause rivalry"; tsror is a piel infinitive of the verb tsarar ("to be hostile to, to distress, to be an adversary"); the related noun tsarah [צָרָה] means "adversity, distress" — hence tsarah is also the word for a rival wife ("adversary-wife"); the hiphil/piel li-tsror: "to create hostility/rivalry"; LXX: ζηλοῦν (zēloun, "to make jealous, to provoke to jealousy"), using ζηλόω (zēloō, "to be zealous/jealous, to provoke jealousy") — the LXX captures the emotional dynamic: jealousy, zeal, rivalry]; to uncover her nakedness [le-gallot ervatah] [le-gallot ervatah — "to uncover her nakedness"; the purpose clause; LXX: ἀποκαλύψαι τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (apokalypsai tēn aschēmosynēn autēs)] beside her [alehah] [alehah — "upon her / upon/in addition to her / while she is alive"; 'alehah can mean "upon her, against her, over her"; in context, "in her lifetime, while she lives" — the prohibition is specifically of taking the sister as a rival while the first sister-wife is still alive; LXX: ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς ζώσης (ep' autēs zōsēs, "upon her while she is living") — the LXX makes the temporal condition explicit: "while she is alive," which is implied but not stated in the MT; the LXX adds ζώσης ("living"), which is a genuine interpretive expansion] in her lifetime [be-chayehah] [be-chayehah — "in her lifetime / while she lives"; be- + chayehah ("her life"): "in her life, during her life"; this closing phrase confirms the LXX's interpretive expansion; after the death of the first wife, the sister may apparently be taken; LXX: ἐν τῇ ζωῇ αὐτῆς (en tē zōē autēs, "in her life")]:

---

**19** And to a woman [ve-el-ishah] [ve-el-ishah — "and to a woman"; the transition from the kin-prohibitions (vv.7–18) to the community violations (vv.19–23); LXX: Καὶ πρὸς γυναῖκα (Kai pros gynaika, "and to a woman")] in her menstrual separation [be-niddat tum'atah] [be-niddat tum'atah — "in her menstrual impurity / in her separation-impurity"; niddah [נִדָּה] = "menstrual impurity, monthly separation"; from nadad ("to wander, to be excluded, to be separated"); tum'atah = "her impurity" (from tame', "impure"); be-niddat = "during the [period of] separation"; the full phrase = "in/during her period of impurity through separation"; the niddah is the woman's menstrual state, during which she and anything she touches are ritually impure (Lev 15:19–30); LXX: ἐν χωρισμῷ ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς (en chōrismō akatharsias autēs, "in the separation of her impurity"), using χωρισμός (chōrismos, "separation, setting apart") and ἀκαθαρσία (akatharsia, "impurity, uncleanness")] you shall not approach [lo' tiqrav] [lo' tiqrav — "you shall not approach"; the same idiom as v.6; LXX: οὐ προσελεύσῃ (ou proseleusei, "you shall not approach")] to uncover her nakedness [le-gallot ervatah] [le-gallot ervatah — "to uncover her nakedness"; LXX: ἀποκαλύψαι τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην αὐτῆς (apokalypsai tēn aschēmosynēn autēs, "to uncover her nakedness")]:

**20** And to the wife of your neighbor [ve-el-eshet amitekha] [ve-el-eshet amitekha — "and to the wife of your neighbor / to the wife of your fellow-man"; amitekha [עֲמִיתֶךָ] = "your companion, your fellow, your associate" (from 'am, "people"; with suffix: "one of your people, your countryman, your fellow"); distinct from the closer re'a ("friend, neighbor") but in the semantic range of one's fellow Israelite; LXX: καὶ πρὸς τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ πλησίον σου (kai pros tēn gynaika tou plēsion sou, "and to the wife of your neighbor"), using πλησίον (plēsion, "neighbor, one near")] you shall not give your emission [lo' titen shekhovtekha] [lo' titen shekhovtekha le-zera' — "you shall not give your lying-down for seed / you shall not give your seminal emission"; titen = "you shall give"; shekhovtekha = "your lying-down, your cohabitation"; from shakav ("to lie [with someone]"); shekhovtekha = "your lying-down" as a noun (from the same root; also "your emission," as the lying-down metonymically represents the sexual act and its emission); le-zera' = "for seed / as seed"; zera' [זֶרַע] = "seed, semen, offspring"; the full phrase lo' titen shekhovtekha le-zera' = "you shall not give your seed-lying-to seed" = "you shall not deposit semen through cohabitation"; the idiom is anatomically precise; LXX: οὐ δώσεις τὴν κοίτην σου εἰς σπέρμα (ou dōseis tēn koitēn sou eis sperma, "you shall not give your bed/cohabitation as seed"), using κοίτη (koitē, "bed, cohabitation, sexual intercourse") and σπέρμα (sperma, "seed, semen")] to become impure [le-tum'ah lakh] [le-tum'ah lakh — "to become impure through it / for defilement for you"; le-tum'ah = "for/as impurity"; lakh = "to you" (dative of result); LXX: ἐπὶ μιασμόν (epi miasmon, "unto defilement"), using μιασμός (miasmos, "pollution, defilement, corruption")]:

**21** And from your seed [u-mi-zar'akha] [u-mi-zar'akha — "and from your seed / and from your offspring/descendants"; zar'akha = "your seed" (from zera', "seed, offspring"); mi- = "from"; LXX: καὶ ἀπὸ τοῦ σπέρματός σου (kai apo tou spermatos sou, "and from your seed")] you shall not give [lo' titen] [lo' titen — "you shall not give / you shall not hand over"; qal imperfect; LXX: οὐ δώσεις (ou dōseis, "you shall not give")] to pass through [la-ha'avir] [la-ha'avir — "to pass through / to cause to pass through"; hiphil infinitive of 'avar ("to pass, to cross over"): "to cause to cross over, to make pass through"; the idiom la-ha'avir la-Molekh ("to cause to pass through to Molech") is the technical term for the Molech rite; the precise nature of the rite is debated: most interpreters understand it as child sacrifice by fire; others understand it as a rite of dedication or passage not necessarily involving death; the verb 'avar ("to pass through") may suggest passing the child through fire (cf. Deut 18:10: "who passes his son or daughter through fire"); the LXX, Deuteronomy, and 2 Kings 17:17, 23:10 all understand the rite as involving fire and death; LXX: ἄρχοντι (archonti, "to the ruler/prince") — the LXX's remarkable rendering: it translates la-Molekh not as a proper name but as "to the ruler/prince," using ἄρχων (archōn, "ruler, chief, prince"); this is a significant interpretive decision: the LXX apparently understood Molekh as a title ("the king, the ruler") rather than as a proper name of a deity, and rendered it with the Greek word for "ruler"; modern scholarship has debated whether Molekh is a proper deity name, a vocalization of melekh ("king") with the vowels of boshet ("shame"), or the name of a type of offering (MLK offering); the LXX's rendering as "ruler/prince" preserves the "king" etymology of the name] to the Molekh [la-Molekh] [la-Molekh — "to the Molekh / to Molech"; Molekh [מֹלֶךְ] is the name/title of the deity to whom children are passed; the Hebrew Molekh is likely a deliberate vocalization of melekh ("king") with the vowels of boshet ("shame") — a polemical reading designed to stigmatize the deity; Molekh worship is associated with Tophet in the Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna) near Jerusalem (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31, 32:35); the worship apparently involved passing children through or into fire; LXX: see above] — and you shall not profane [ve-lo' techalel] [ve-lo' techalel — "and you shall not profane / and you shall not desecrate"; piel of chalal: "to pierce, to bore through, to profane, to desecrate"; the piel techalel = "you shall profane, you shall hollow out/desecrate"; the act of giving children to Molekh desecrates the divine Name; LXX: καὶ οὐ βεβηλώσεις (kai ou bebēlōseis, "and you shall not profane/desecrate"), using βεβηλόω (bebēloō, "to profane, to desecrate, to make common/worldly")] the name of your God [et-shem Eloheikha] [et-shem Eloheikha — "the name of your God"; shem = "name"; Eloheikha = "your God" (singular possessive — "your God" addressed to the individual Israelite); the Divine Name is the covenant-identity of YHWH; to desecrate it is to damage the relational connection between YHWH and his people; LXX: τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ θεοῦ σου (to onoma tou theou sou, "the name of your God")]. I am the LORD [ani YHWH] [ani YHWH — "I am the LORD"; the divine self-identification; LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος (egō eimi kyrios, "I am the LORD")]:

**22** And with a male [ve-et-zakhar] [ve-et-zakhar — "and with a male / and as for a male"; et = accusative marker; zakhar [זָכָר] = "male, man" (as distinct from ishah, "woman/wife"); the term zakhar denotes biological maleness (cf. Gen 1:27: zakhar u-neqevah, "male and female"); LXX: καὶ μετὰ ἄρσενος (kai meta arsenos, "and with a male"), using ἄρσην (arsēn, "male")] you shall not lie [lo' tishkav] [lo' tishkav — "you shall not lie"; qal imperfect of shakav: "to lie, to lie with (sexually)"; the verb is used throughout Genesis and Samuel for sexual intercourse (shakav 'im / et); LXX: οὐ κοιμηθήσῃ (ou koimēthēsē, "you shall not lie down"), using κοιμάομαι (koimaomai, "to sleep, to lie down")] as the lyings of a woman [mishkevei ishah] [mishkevei ishah — "as the lyings of a woman / as one lies with a woman"; mishkevei = "lyings of" (construct plural of mishkav, "lying down, bed, act of lying"); ishah = "a woman"; the idiom mishkevei ishah = "as one lies with a woman" — the noun mishkav in the plural (mishkevei) idiomatically denotes the manner/type of sexual intercourse associated with a woman; the verse prohibits a man from lying with another man in the manner in which one lies with a woman; LXX: κοίτην γυναικείαν (koitēn gynaikein, "a woman's bed/lying"), using κοίτη (koitē, "bed, cohabitation") and γυναικεῖος (gynaikeios, "of/for a woman, feminine")] — it is an abomination [to'evah hi'] [to'evah hi' — "it is an abomination / it is a detestable thing"; to'evah [תּוֹעֵבָה] = "abomination, detestable practice, something morally/ritually repugnant" (from ta'av, "to abhor, to be disgusted with"); to'evah is the strongest term of moral-religious condemnation in the Levitical vocabulary; it appears 117 times in the Hebrew Bible (43 in Ezekiel alone); in Leviticus the term is used exclusively for sexual prohibitions (18:22, 26, 27, 29, 30; 20:13) and for dishonest weights (Deut 25:16) and idolatry (Deut 7:25); hi' = "it is" (the feminine pronoun making to'evah the predicate); LXX: βδέλυγμά ἐστιν (bdelygma estin, "it is an abomination/detestable thing"), using βδέλυγμα (bdelygma, "abomination, abhorrence, abominable thing") — βδέλυγμα is the standard LXX rendering of to'evah and carries the full moral-religious weight of the Hebrew]:

**23** And with any animal [u-ve-khol-behemah] [u-ve-khol-behemah — "and with any animal / and with every beast"; be- = "with"; khol = "every, any"; behemah [בְּהֵמָה] = "beast, animal" — specifically domesticated livestock (as distinct from chayah, "wild animal"); though in context it likely covers all animals; LXX: καὶ πρὸς πᾶν τετράπουν (kai pros pan tetraoun, "and with any four-footed creature"), using τετράπους (tetrapous, "four-footed, quadruped")] you shall not give your emission [lo' titen shekhovtekha] [lo' titen shekhovtekha — as in v.20; "you shall not give your lying-down / your cohabitation"; the same idiom for sexual emission; LXX: οὐ δώσεις τὴν κοίτην σου (ou dōseis tēn koitēn sou, "you shall not give your bed/cohabitation")] to become impure by it [le-tum'ah vo] [le-tum'ah vo — "to become impure through/by it"; as in v.20; vo = "by it / in it" (masculine suffix — referring to the animal); LXX: εἰς σπερματισμόν (eis spermatismon, "for seminal emission"), using σπερματισμός (spermatismos, "seminal emission") — the LXX is more anatomically explicit here; MT: "to become impure through it"]. And a woman [ve-ishah] [ve-ishah — "and a woman"; the female subject, introduced mid-verse: the prohibition applies equally to women with animals; LXX: καὶ γυνή (kai gynē, "and a woman")] — she shall not stand before an animal [lo' ta'amod lifnei behemah] [lo' ta'amod lifnei behemah — "she shall not stand before an animal"; ta'amod = "she shall stand" (qal imperfect of 'amad); lifnei = "before, in front of"; the phrase "standing before an animal" is the idiom for presenting herself for intercourse with the animal; LXX: οὐ στήσεται πρὸς κτῆνος (ou stēsetai pros ktēnos, "she shall not stand/present herself to a beast"), using κτῆνος (ktēnos, "cattle, beast, animal")] to lie with it [le-ravah 'alav] [le-ravah 'alav — "to lie with it / to be mounted by it"; le-ravah = piel infinitive of ravah: "to cause to lie down, to mount (said of a male animal mating with a female)"; the piel (revi'ah) in this context means "to be/let oneself be mounted by" or "to copulate with (a male animal mating position)"; 'alav = "upon it / by it" (masculine suffix); the term here is sexually explicit; LXX: ὀχευθῆναι (ocheuthenai, "to be covered/mounted [by a male animal]"), using ὀχεύω (ocheuō, "to mount, to cover a female in animal mating") — the LXX uses the technical Greek term for animal copulation] — it is a perversion [tevel hi'] [tevel hi' — "it is a perversion / it is a confusion"; tevel [תֶּבֶל] = "confusion, mixing, perversion"; from balal ("to mix, to confuse, to blend improperly"); the only biblical occurrence of tevel as a condemnatory noun (tevel also appears as a name for "the world" in Ps 9:9, etc., but that is a different word); as a moral term, tevel = the wrong mixing of categories: human and animal, species boundaries crossed; LXX: ἀσέβεια γάρ ἐστιν (asebeia gar estin, "for it is impiety/godlessness"), using ἀσέβεια (asebeia, "ungodliness") — the LXX adds γάρ ("for") and renders tevel as ἀσέβεια ("impiety") rather than as "perversion/confusion"; the LXX theologizes where the MT categorizes]:

---

**24** You shall not make yourselves impure [al-titame'u] [al-titame'u — "do not make yourselves impure / do not defile yourselves"; al + hithpael imperative (titame'u) of tame' ("to be impure, to defile"): "do not become defiled through/in any of these"; hithpael = reflexive: "cause yourselves to be impure"; LXX: μὴ μολύνεσθε (mē molyneste, "do not defile yourselves"), using μολύνω (molynō, "to defile, to pollute, to soil")] in any of all these [be-khol-eleh] [be-khol-eleh — "in any of all these / through any of these things"; referring back to the list of prohibited acts in vv.7–23; LXX: ἐν πᾶσι τούτοις (en pasi toutois, "in all these things")] — for in all these [ki be-khol-eleh] [ki be-khol-eleh — "for in all these"; the causal explanation begins; LXX: ὅτι ἐν πᾶσι τούτοις (hoti en pasi toutois, "for in all these things")] the nations were made impure [nitme'u ha-goyim] [nitme'u ha-goyim — "the nations were made impure / the peoples defiled themselves"; nitme'u = niphal perfect of tame': "were defiled, became impure, made themselves impure"; ha-goyim = "the nations, the peoples" (non-Israelite); LXX: ἐμιάνθησαν τὰ ἔθνη (emianthēsan ta ethnē, "the nations were polluted/defiled"), using μιαίνω (miainō, "to pollute, to contaminate, to defile")] whom I am sending out before you [asher ani meshalleach mi-peneikhem] [asher ani meshalleach mi-peneikhem — "whom I am driving out before you / whom I am sending away from your face"; meshalleach = piel participle of shalaach ("to send, to send away, to drive out, to expel"); mi-peneikhem = "from before you, from your face"; the divine act of dispossessing the Canaanite nations is already in process in the divine perspective; LXX: ἃ ἐγὼ ἐξαποστέλλω πρὸ προσώπου ὑμῶν (ha egō exapostellō pro prosōpou hymōn, "which I am sending away before your face"), using ἐξαποστέλλω (exapostellō, "to send away, to dismiss, to dispatch")]:

**25** And the land was made impure [va-titma' ha-aretz] [va-titma' ha-aretz — "and the land became impure / and the land was defiled"; qal imperfect with waw-consecutive: a completed past action with narrative force; ha-aretz = "the land, the earth"; the land itself (not just the inhabitants) is the subject of ritual defilement; the Hebrew theology of the land is remarkable: the land is not merely territory but a living covenantal entity that absorbs and responds to the moral conduct of its inhabitants; LXX: καὶ ἐμιάνθη ἡ γῆ (kai emianthē hē gē, "and the land was defiled"), using μιαίνω (miainō, "to defile")] and I visited its iniquity upon it [va-efqod avonah alehah] [va-efqod avonah alehah — "and I visited its iniquity upon it / and I accounted its iniquity against it"; ## Translator's Notes — Key Terms and Exegetical Issues

### 1. Va-chai bahem — "He shall live by them" (v.5)

The phrase va-chai bahem is the theological pivot of the chapter's opening frame and one of the most cited verses in the entire Pauline corpus. The Hebrew is syntactically brief: va-chai ("and he shall live") + bahem ("in/by/through them"). Three debates surround the phrase:

(a) **What kind of "life"?** The context in Leviticus is most naturally the life of covenant fidelity — the Israelite who observes these statutes will live, will survive, will flourish as a member of the covenant people. Ezekiel's three citations (20:11, 13, 21) use the verse to mark each generation's failure: YHWH gave his statutes "that the one who does them might live by them," and each generation refused. This makes the life in view primarily covenantal-national life.

(b) **The Pauline tension.** Paul in Galatians 3:12 cites the verse as the antithesis to the Habakkuk 2:4 principle ("the righteous shall live by faith"): "the law is not of faith, but 'the one who does them shall live by them.'" Paul is not dismissing the verse's claim — he is treating it as a genuine but humanly unattainable standard that requires a different mechanism of life (faith in Messiah). In Romans 10:5 Paul attributes the same verse to Moses as the description of "the righteousness based on the law."

(c) **Translation precision.** The preposition ba- in bahem is ambiguous: "in them" (sphere), "by them" (instrument), "through them" (means). EMET renders "by them" to preserve the instrumental force.

### 2. Lo' tiqrevu le-gallot ervah — "You shall not approach to uncover nakedness" (v.6)

The idiom gallot ervah ("to uncover nakedness") is the chapter's governing phrase, occurring 24 times. Galah (piel: "to uncover, to strip, to expose, to reveal") + ervah ("nakedness, genitalia, sexual shame") creates a euphemism that is simultaneously clinical and moral: it names the act by its physical component (the uncovering of what should be covered) while investing it with shame (ervah carries the negative connotation of shameful exposure). The prohibition lo' tiqrevu ("you shall not approach") pushes the boundary back before the act itself — the approach, the movement toward uncovering, is itself prohibited. This is the Levitical logic of the "fence": the command does not merely forbid the final act but creates a distance requirement. The LXX renders this consistently as apokalypsai aschēmosynēn ("to reveal indecency"), where ἀσχημοσύνη ("indecency, unseemliness") adds an evaluative layer absent from the relatively neutral Hebrew ervah.

### 3. La-ha'avir la-Molekh — "To pass through to Molech" (v.21)

The Molech prohibition stands at the center of a puzzling sequence: sexual violations in vv.6–20, the Molech rite in v.21, male homosexual intercourse in v.22, and bestiality in v.23. Why is Molech in this list? Several proposals:

(a) The Molech rite involves the "seed" (zera') — children — just as vv.19–20 involve the male "emission" (zera'); the thematic link is the misuse of zera', whether as semen deposited in the wrong place or as children offered to a foreign deity.

(b) The rite desecrates YHWH's Name (v.21b) — a specifically covenantal violation that connects to the covenant-identity formula ani YHWH framing the chapter.

(c) The Molech rite violates the boundary between YHWH's domain (Israelite children belong to YHWH) and a foreign deity's demand.

The LXX renders la-Molekh as "to the ruler/archon" (ἄρχοντι), interpreting Molekh as a title (melekh = king/ruler) rather than a proper name. Modern scholarship remains divided on whether Molekh is (i) a deity name, (ii) a vocalization of melekh ("king") with boshet ("shame") vowels, or (iii) the name of a type of offering (the MLK offering of the Punic texts). EMET preserves "Molekh" as the standard transliteration of the Hebrew.

### 4. Mishkevei ishah — "As the lyings of a woman" (v.22)

The phrase mishkevei ishah ("the lyings of a woman") is syntactically unusual. The plural construct mishkevei ("lyings of") does not refer to multiple acts but to the category of sexual act associated with women — specifically, the penetrative posture that characterizes male-female intercourse. The prohibition thus specifies a particular act (male anal penetration of another male) rather than making a broader statement about same-sex attraction or orientation. The term to'evah ("abomination") applied to this act is the same term applied to Canaanite cultic practices (v.27), dishonest scales (Deut 25:16), and idolatry (Deut 7:25). It is the strongest evaluative negative in the Levitical vocabulary.

### 5. Va-taqiya ha-aretz — "The land vomited out" (v.25)

The vomiting-land image is unique to Leviticus 18 and 20 (20:22). It draws on the ancient Near Eastern concept of the land as a living entity with a covenant relationship to its inhabitants. In this theological framework, the land is not passive geography but a participant in covenant: it observes the behavior of its inhabitants, accumulates the defilement of their sins, and eventually acts — expelling them as a body expels a toxic substance. The verb qi' ("to vomit") is used of a sick body and of the sea throwing out Jonah (Jonah 2:10 [MT 2:11]); its use for the land's expulsion of the Canaanites (and the threatened expulsion of Yisra'el) makes the land a character in the moral drama of history. The image carries a warning: Yisra'el is not immune; the land will vomit them out too if they repeat Canaan's pattern (v.28).

### 6. Tevel — "Perversion/Confusion" (v.23)

Tevel appears only here as a moral condemnatory term. Its root balal ("to mix, to confuse") connects it to the Babel story (Gen 11:9 LXX variant) and to the general biblical concern with boundaries between created categories. Sexual intercourse with an animal crosses the most fundamental created boundary: the separation of humanity (made in the image of God) from the animal world. The term tevel thus condemns not merely the act but the category violation it represents — the "mixing" or "confusion" of what was created distinct.

### 7. Chuqqot ha-to'evot — "The statutes of the abominations" (v.30)

The closing verse contains a striking rhetorical irony: YHWH commands Yisra'el to keep "my charge" (mishmarti) and to not do from "the statutes of the abominations" (chuqqot ha-to'evot). The word chuqqot is the same word used for YHWH's own "statutes" (vv.4, 5, 26). The Canaanite abominations are also chuqqot — they too have their prescribed, engraved patterns of behavior. The juxtaposition is deliberate: Yisra'el faces a choice between two sets of chuqqot, YHWH's and Canaan's. The chapter began with this contrast (v.3: "do not walk in their chuqqot") and ends by naming what those Canaanite chuqqot are: to'evot, abominations. The frame is complete.

## LXX Divergences (Vaticanus, pp. 104–105)

1. **v.5:** LXX adds "your God" (ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν) to "I am the LORD," making the closing formula fuller than MT's ani YHWH alone.

2. **v.7:** LXX renders ervat imekha ("nakedness of your mother") without the copular assertion "she is your mother" in the same explicit form; it restructures slightly.

3. **v.9 (end):** LXX uses singular feminine "her nakedness" (αὐτῆς) for MT's plural "their nakedness" (ervatam) — a minor MS variation suggesting different Vorlage or scribal harmonization.

4. **v.18 (end):** LXX adds ζώσης ("living, while she is alive") making explicit the temporal condition that is implicit in MT's be-chayehah. The LXX supplements: "upon her while she is living" — the MT's be-chayehah follows later.

5. **v.21:** LXX renders la-Molekh as ἄρχοντι ("to the ruler/prince") rather than transliterating as a proper name — a significant theological-interpretive decision that refuses to treat Molekh as a deity-name, reducing him to a human "ruler."

6. **v.23 (woman's prohibition):** LXX renders tevel as ἀσέβεια ("impiety/godlessness"), adding γάρ ("for"), and thus theologizes what the MT expresses as a categorical violation (mixing/confusion).

7. **v.25:** LXX uses ἀνταπέδωκα ("I repaid/recompensed") for MT's va-efqod ("I visited/charged"), shifting from the juridical "visitation" vocabulary to the recompense vocabulary.

8. **v.26:** LXX adds πάντα ("all") to "my statutes" — "all my ordinances" — not present in MT.

9. **v.30:** LXX renders mishmarti ("my charge") as ta prostagmata mou ("my commands"), losing the specific mishmereth concept of "entrusted charge/watch."
